rights.basis: The pinned CME submodule carries an Unlicense worded for software, while the General Corpus README separately licenses code under GPL-3.0 and non-public-domain output under CC BY-SA 4.0. The imported XML also preserves an older personal-scholarly-use notice, and its identified OTA source now has CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 terms. No file-specific statement reconciles those layers, so no single repository license is asserted as controlling every textual and markup component.
rights.component: Checked-in XML serialization, transcription, and modern encoded labor

notes: A United States no-copyright statement is not a worldwide open license and does not license manuscript photographs or the modern source edition.

rights.basis: The checked-in U-M header says the text was made available through OTA ‘for personal scholarly use only.’ This historical restriction conflicts in scope with the current U-M No Copyright-United States display and current OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 deposit license; no item-specific reconciliation was found.
rights.component: Legacy availability notice embedded in the 1993 U-M XML header
notes: The notice is preserved as historically explicit evidence, not silently treated as revoked or as controlling unrelated manuscript images and editions.

entities.description: The public CME presentation and structured XML transformation of the Otho stream. It numbers 14,560 L rows, supplies 146 100-line navigation groups and 412 Brook-Leslie page milestones, and converts the OTA custom-character notation to Unicode and normalized delimiters.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME digital text LayOtho
notes: The header says front and back matter, editorial notes, and printed illustrations from the source edition were omitted.

After milestone whitespace normalization, 14,559 of 14,560 U-M rows match current OTA 1682 Otho. At row 7574 U-M has ‘Þus endede par mid mochele wowe Vortiger.’ while OTA has ‘Þus endede þar; mid mochele wowe Vortiger.’; the responsible correction or corruption stage is undocumented.

rights.basis: The current item page states that the materials are in the public domain and displays the DPLA No Copyright-United States statement. The scope of that display over independently protectable markup and metadata is not separately explained.

access.accessMethod: Locate the two volumes through CiNii or WorldCat and request them from a holding library or via interlibrary loan.

bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 250, 277
bibliographic.title: Laȝamon: Brut

entities.description: The immediate two-volume source edition, presenting distinct Caligula and Otho streams from Cotton Caligula A IX and Cotton Otho C XIII. The target electronic payload selects its Otho stream and excludes the edition's front/back matter, notes, and illustrations.
entities.label: G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie, eds., Laȝamon: Brut (1963-1978)
notes: The exact introductions and apparatus remain controlled-access; the public volume-1 metadata does not reveal every normalization, supply, or collation rule.
The XML says printed illustrations were not represented electronically. Their exact inventory requires inspection of both print volumes.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1963 and 1978 and no open license or public-domain assertion for its modern editorial contributions was found. The available volume-1 scan is controlled/print-disabled rather than openly downloadable.
rights.component: Modern editorial text, apparatus, introductions, notes, and facsimile matter in the 1963 and 1978 volumes
notes: No exact term expiry is inferred because relevant authorship, employer, and publication facts were not fully established.

access.accessMethod: Restrictions apply. Register for a Reader Pass, request the shelfmark through the online form, and consult British Library staff before travel; manuscripts and archives are consulted in London.
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII

access.accessMethod: Submit the online Imaging Services order for Cotton MS Otho C XIII and request color expert photography of the complete present physical object: both boards/covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, fragments, edges if offered, and every surviving manuscript leaf. Give the work-to-folio crosswalk separately after institutional confirmation.
access.contact: permissions@bl.uk (current Imaging Services terms); licensing@bl.uk (digitisation page)
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII
access.format: Commissioned color photography or an available surrogate; exact format and coverage subject to conservation review and quote

localCopies.label: British Library digitisation and reproduction price list
notes: The exact two-page provider PDF was retained byte-for-byte. qpdf opens it and reports a valid page tree but warns about recoverable linearization/shared-hint offsets; pdftotext extracts both pages successfully, so no repair was made.

Published 2025/26 rates include photographic TIFF at £27.70 for the first image, £7.25 per subsequent image, and £58.19 per complex-handling image. A fragile 144-folio whole-object project may fall outside standard rates and requires an institutional quote.

The service says original manuscripts cannot be scanned; rare or precious items may require expert photography. If a surrogate or microfilm is used, explicitly request color because surrogate scans may be black and white.
The current digitisation page and January 2026 Imaging Services terms list different permission email aliases; preserve the provider's written response and controlling terms with any delivered files.

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms updated January 2026 say supplied material may be subject to rights and must not be copied or reproduced without written British Library permission; the service page separately directs reproduction enquiries to licensing staff.
rights.component: Any commissioned British Library photographs, scans from surrogates, delivery files, and reproduction rights
rights.jurisdiction: Provider contract and intended place of reuse
notes: A quote or delivery does not by itself authorize Git redistribution, research publication, or commercial/print-on-demand use. Preserve the written permission and exact covered files.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: Fourth quarter of the thirteenth century (1275-1299)

entities.description: The surviving witness of the later abridged and reworked Otho version. It is an English parchment manuscript of the fourth quarter of the thirteenth century, extensively damaged in the 1731 Cotton Library fire. The exact current pencil-folio crosswalk to the electronic rows awaits a complete institutional surrogate.
entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII
notes: The official catalog describes this as one item and gives no current digitised-content link. That dated negative finding does not prove that private photography, microfilm, or unindexed legacy images do not exist.

physicalDescription.format: Manuscript of the Otho version of Laȝamon's Brut
notes: Leaves were damaged in the 1731 Cotton Library fire; the manuscript was rebound by the British Museum in 1962.

The current catalog records 144 folios. Madden reported 145 surviving leaves in 1847 and discussed still earlier 154/155-folio counts; these are dated historical states, not silently reconciled with present foliation.
Madden described one hand, double columns, 38 lines, colored initials, severe damage in the first fifty leaves, and additional missing or injured text.

rights.basis: The represented manuscript and text were produced in the thirteenth century. This status is separate from ownership, access restrictions, fire-damage documentation, photography, and provider terms.
rights.component: Underlying medieval text and manuscript content, excluding any modern photography and catalog text

rights.basis: British Library Imaging Services terms updated January 2026 say supplied material may be subject to rights and must not be copied or reproduced without written British Library permission; the service page separately directs reproduction enquiries to licensing staff.

rights.jurisdiction: Provider contract and intended place of reuse

bibliographic.citation: Laȝamons Brut, or Chronicle of Britain: A Poetical Semi-Saxon Paraphrase of the Brut of Wace, ed. Frederic Madden, 3 vols. (London: Society of Antiquaries of London, 1847).
bibliographic.title: Laȝamons Brut, or Chronicle of Britain

entities.description: Madden's three-volume parallel presentation of the Caligula and Otho texts with literal translation, notes, glossary, and a selected Otho manuscript plate. The later electronic convention attributes single-parenthesized now-illegible readings specifically to this edition.
entities.label: Frederic Madden, ed., Laȝamons Brut, or Chronicle of Britain (1847)
notes: Volume 1's preface calls Otho an abbreviated recension and gives a dated account of its 1731 fire damage, restoration, historical folio counts, missing leaves, and damaged text.
The selected Otho plate appears at PDF page 47 / printed preface page xxxviii in the cached volume 1. It is not a complete facsimile and its exact current folio was not verified.

rights.basis: The three-volume edition was published in 1847 and is historical public-domain material in the United States.
rights.component: Underlying 1847 publication text, translation, notes, and glossary

dateStatements.value: Second half of the thirteenth century (1250-1299)

entities.description: The longer, more conservative Caligula-version witness. It is collateral to the target Otho version and useful for version comparison, but it is not treated as an unrestricted source of Otho wording.

historical_designations: British Museum MS Cotton Caligula A.IX

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Caligula A IX

physicalDescription.dimensions: approximately 215 × 150 mm; text space approximately 170 × 115 mm
physicalDescription.extent: 261 folios, with two modern endleaves and flyleaves; Laȝamon's Brut at ff. 3r-194v
physicalDescription.format: Composite codex written in two columns
notes: Origin assigned to England, south-west Midlands.
The Brut is the most complete surviving copy and opens at f. 3r with the red rubric ‘Incipit historia brutonum.’
The codex also contains unrelated Middle English and Anglo-Norman works outside this work record.

rights.basis: The represented manuscript and text were produced in the thirteenth century. This status is separate from ownership, physical access, photography, and provider terms.

542 image requests used the supported 1200-pixel-width profile; the spine's requested 1200-pixel URL was rejected and the effective full-size fallback is preserved in the inventory.
The collateral Caligula Brut occupies ff. 3r-194v / canvases 15-398; the required work_portion object below labels that different version explicitly and does not describe Cotton Otho C XIII.

next_steps: Consult both printed volumes' introductions, apparatus, and illustration lists through a holding library or controlled borrowing.
Map each electronic symbol category to the edition's exact convention without assuming every Otho supply comes from Caligula.
Model each omitted manuscript plate separately after its target and extent are verified.
openQuestions.question: What exact base-text, collation, supply, normalization, and apparatus rules did Brook and Leslie use for the Otho stream, and what illustrations were omitted from the electronic text?

next_steps: Ask U-M for item-level production metadata, source files, and correction history.
Compare row 7574 with Brook-Leslie and Madden before preferring either reading.
Do not silently correct the repository XML until the responsible layer is established.
openQuestions.question: Who transformed OTA Otho into the 1993 U-M item, from which exact source copy, and why does U-M row 7574 differ from current and legacy OTA?

next_steps: Follow up through Imaging Services and Reference Services with shelfmark Cotton MS Otho C XIII.
Request all present surfaces—boards/covers, spine, pastedowns, flyleaves, blanks, fragments, edges if offered, and every surviving leaf—not merely selected text pages.
After receipt, record exact delivery fixity and map the Otho text to current folios/canvases separately.
openQuestions.question: Can the British Library supply a complete current-object color facsimile, or identify a complete legacy microfilm or photographic surrogate, of Cotton MS Otho C XIII?

next_steps: Preserve the user's institutional response, quote, order confirmation, and exact terms with the delivered-file record.
Ask which permission contact and terms control, because the digitisation page and January 2026 Imaging Services terms list different email aliases.
Do not commit, redistribute, or include commissioned images in publication packages until written permission covers the exact use.
openQuestions.question: What written British Library terms would govern private research storage, Git redistribution, scholarly publication, self-publishing, and commercial/print-on-demand reuse of any commissioned Otho images?

next_steps: Use a complete current surrogate and current pencil foliation as the physical baseline.
Transcribe Madden's missing-leaf discussion and compare Brook-Leslie page/line references.
Retain dated historical counts rather than forcing them into one invented continuous foliation.
openQuestions.question: How do Madden's historical 145-leaf state, the current catalog's 144 folios, missing leaves, and Brook-Leslie/OTA row sequence map to one another?

next_steps: Request item-level clarification from U-M and OTA about transcription and markup.
Ask maintainers to define repository-license scope over imported XML.
Keep medieval text, Brook-Leslie edition, OTA streams, U-M encoding, repository serialization, and generated output separate until clarified.
openQuestions.question: How should the 1993 personal-scholarly-use notice, current U-M No Copyright-United States display, OTA CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 licenses, CME software-worded Unlicense, and General Corpus output license be reconciled for redistribution of this exact XML?

next_steps: Ask the British Library to clarify the exact manifest's ‘Public Domain in most countries other than the UK’ statement and intended reuse.
Keep the complete cache private and Git-ignored pending clarification.
openQuestions.question: What current British Library terms govern redistribution or publication of the cached collateral Caligula IIIF photographs, especially in the United Kingdom?

reviewNotes: Primary path: repository XML → U-M LayOtho → OTA 1682 Otho payload → OTA 0085 Otho stream → Brook-Leslie Otho stream → Cotton Otho C XIII.
All 14,560 legacy 0085 Otho rows match current 1682 after the documented character and delimiter conversion; 14,559 of 14,560 U-M rows then match after milestone whitespace normalization.
Madden 1847 supplies only the explicitly parenthesized now-illegible readings and a selected manuscript plate; neither is a whole-manuscript facsimile or whole-text base.

The complete Caligula IIIF object is a collateral witness to a different version. Its exact ff. 3r-194v / canvas 15-398 mapping is explicitly labeled as a collateral-version work portion, never as the target Otho manuscript.
No complete current public Otho facsimile was verified. A mediated whole-object photographic request, provider quote, and written reuse terms remain necessary.

The imported XML also preserves an older personal-scholarly-use notice, and its identified OTA source now has CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 terms. No file-specific statement reconciles those layers, so no single repository license is asserted as controlling every textual and markup component.

notes: The medieval text's age does not automatically resolve rights in modern editorial selection, apparatus, markup, or repository-created output.

Published 2025/26 rates include photographic TIFF at £27.70 for the first image, £7.25 per subsequent image, and £58.19 per complex-handling image. A fragile 144-folio whole-object project may fall outside standard rates and requires an institutional quote.

The service says original manuscripts cannot be scanned; rare or precious items may require expert photography. If a surrogate or microfilm is used, explicitly request color because surrogate scans may be black and white.
The current digitisation page and January 2026 Imaging Services terms list different permission email aliases; preserve the provider's written response and controlling terms with any delivered files.

lineage.summary: The repository XML represents the Otho version of Laȝamon's Brut, not an undifferentiated text of both surviving versions. Its primary path is General Corpus LayOtho.lineated.xml → the 1993 U-M CME item LayOtho → Oxford Text Archive deposit 1682's Otho payload → the August 1988 OTA 0085 Otho stream → G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie's 1963-1978 EETS Otho text → British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII. Character-normalized comparison matches all 14,560 legacy and current OTA rows; U-M differs substantively at only row 7574 after page-milestone whitespace is removed. The electronic convention also preserves selected now-illegible readings from Madden's 1847 parallel edition. Cotton Otho C XIII is a fire-damaged 144-folio single-work manuscript with no complete current public facsimile found; the British Library catalog and reproduction route are recorded without claiming nonexistence. Madden's complete three-volume edition scans, its selected Otho plate, OTA deliverables, Brook-Leslie metadata, the current full Caligula comparison facsimile, rights assertions, and cache fixity remain separate layers.

catalogSummary.author: Laȝamon; anonymous Otho reviser
catalogSummary.date: Otho revision before or during production of the surviving witness (1275–1299); exact revision date unknown
catalogSummary.editor: G. L. Brook and R. F. Leslie
catalogSummary.region: England for the surviving Otho witness; exact place of revision unresolved
catalogSummary.title: Laȝamon’s Brut (Otho version)

catalogingSubject.label: Laȝamon’s Brut (Otho version)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The cataloged unit is the abridged and reworked Otho version of Laȝamon’s Middle English verse history, represented by British Library Cotton MS Otho C XIII and by the 14,560-row Brook-Leslie/OTA/U-M Otho stream. It is not the abstract work embracing both versions, the longer Caligula version, either physical codex, the 1847 or 1963-1978 editions, the electronic deposits, or the General Corpus XML artifact. Fire damage and editorial damage markers make encoded-row count different from recoverable verse extent; the XML's 146 supplied 100-line groups and 412 printed-page milestones are navigation rather than authorial divisions.

contentParts.label: Laȝamon’s Brut, Otho version

locators.value: entire surviving manuscript; 144 folios in the current catalog, exact text-to-pencil-foliation crosswalk pending

notes: No lower-level authorial contents structure is asserted. The XML's 146 supplied divisions are intentionally not represented as intellectual parts.

The first leaf and several later leaves were already missing or damaged in Madden's nineteenth-century account; exact present folio boundaries must be established from a complete current surrogate or institutional response.
contentParts.summary: The surviving Otho-version stream represented by the repository encoding, with extensive lacuna and damage notation.

dateStatements.display: Before or during production of the surviving Otho witness (1275–1299); exact revision date unknown
notes: The upper bound reflects the surviving witness, not proof that revision occurred in 1299. The underlying Brut was probably composed in the later twelfth century and before 1216, but that broader-work date is not silently assigned as the date of this version.

dateStatements.display: Fourth quarter of the thirteenth century (1275–1299), in Cotton MS Otho C XIII
notes: This is the date of the surviving Otho manuscript, not the date of Laȝamon's underlying composition.

extent.description: One counted verse work. The repository XML contains 14,560 unique consecutively numbered L rows, 12,426 letter-bearing rows, 2,134 marker-only rows, 146 DIV1 elements explicitly labeled supplied line groups, and 412 PB milestones for odd-numbered source-edition pages 3-839. The verse_lines value is therefore a transparent count of letter-bearing encoded rows, not a claim for the complete intrinsic length of the damaged Otho version.

formStatements.description: A long Middle English verse history whose long lines combine alliteration and rhyme. The repository XML has 14,560 numbered L rows, of which 12,426 contain letters; 2,134 are marker-only rows recording additions, lacunae, or damage rather than recoverable verse text.

genres.label: Historiography
notes: The poem reshapes legendary and historical traditions; the label does not imply modern critical historiography.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: Latin bibliographic title forms and modern English editorial metadata do not make the medieval verse itself multilingual.
The Caligula and Otho texts are versions of one abstract work but materially different textual streams; this record deliberately catalogs only Otho.

The Otho manuscript's copy date and region, the underlying Brut's probable composition period, the Brook-Leslie edition dates, the OTA dates, and the 1993 U-M date describe different layers and are not merged.
The 14,560 numbered electronic rows are practical locators. Marker-only rows and fire damage prevent that figure from serving as a simple intrinsic verse-line total.
Image availability, commissioned-image terms, modern-edition copyright, and digital-encoding rights are recorded by component in the lineage manifest; none is inferred from the medieval text's age.

next_steps: Consult Christopher Cannon's work on style and authorship and Elizabeth Bryan's Otho study alongside newer scholarship.
Keep the anonymous-reviser label collective and descriptive until passage-level agency is demonstrated.
Do not infer revision date from the witness date more narrowly than the evidence allows.
openQuestions.question: When and by whom was the Otho version abridged and reworked, and which differences reflect deliberate redaction rather than copying variation or later physical loss?

next_steps: Request a complete color photographic surrogate of all available surfaces from the British Library and ask for the current pencil foliation.
Compare the delivered surrogate with Madden 1847 and Brook-Leslie before adding exact folio boundaries.
Do not invent a continuous 1r-144v work range from the catalog's folio count.
openQuestions.question: How do the 14,560 electronic rows and Brook-Leslie page milestones map to the current 144-folio Otho object, including lost and fire-damaged leaves?

next_steps: Review Brook and Leslie's introductions and a complete Otho surrogate.
Do not convert the 146 supplied XML line groups into chapters.
Add stable intellectual parts only when manuscript or editorial evidence establishes their boundaries.
openQuestions.question: What large-scale divisions, if any, can be attributed to the Otho version rather than to Brook-Leslie lineation or U-M navigation?

next_steps: Inspect a complete Otho surrogate or obtain an institutional folio reference.
Keep historical Latin title forms in explanatory notes rather than assigning an unverified Otho original title.
openQuestions.question: What exact Otho-witness rubric, if any, supplies a historical title such as Libri Brutonum, and where does it survive in the damaged manuscript?

notes: The British Library assigns the surviving manuscript an English origin. This does not establish a precise place for the Otho revision.
The narrative ranges beyond Britain but organizes a legendary history of the Britons from Trojan origins to the end of British rule.
This does not assign every Otho wording or abbreviation directly to Laȝamon.

scope.description: Authorship of the underlying Brut inherited by the Otho version

notes: The relation concerns the underlying Brut; the Otho version adds a later layer of revision.

scope.description: Underlying adaptation of Wace's Roman de Brut

notes: Current scholarship characterizes Otho as an abridgement and reworking, but intentional revision, scribal change, and later fire loss must not be conflated passage by passage.

scope.description: Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship
Editorial responsibility for the 1963-1978 immediate source edition, not medieval authorship

subjects.label: Legendary history of Britain and the Britons

scope.description: The Arthurian reign, wars, and fall within the larger Brut history

summaries.value: The Otho version retells the legendary history of Britain from Brutus and Trojan origins through successive rulers, invasions, Arthur's reign and fall, and the ending of British rule. It preserves Laȝamon's verse adaptation of Wace's Roman de Brut in a later, substantially abridged and reworked form whose diction is often modernized. The surviving manuscript is also severely damaged by the 1731 Cotton Library fire, so deliberate condensation, scribal revision, and later physical loss remain distinct explanatory layers.

This record describes the Otho version only. Cotton Otho C XIII is its surviving witness. Cotton Caligula A IX transmits the longer, more conservative version and remains a collateral comparison source, not a coequal witness to every Otho reading. Brook and Leslie's edition prints both streams; OTA and U-M separately encode the Otho stream while omitting edition front matter, back matter, notes, and illustrations.

notes: The parenthetical version label is supplied for discovery and prevents the materially different Caligula version from being collapsed into this record.
The historical institution name and shelfmark punctuation are retained from the U-M discovery title; the current holding institution is the British Library.
titles.value: Layamon's Brut (British Museum Ms. Cotton Otho C.XIII)

notes: Goering identifies Brut as the modern conventional title; the surviving manuscripts also circulate historical Latin title forms, but this review does not assign one to a specific Otho rubric without direct image evidence.

work.summary: The Otho version retells the legendary history of Britain from Brutus and Trojan origins through successive rulers, invasions, Arthur's reign and fall, and the ending of British rule. It preserves Laȝamon's verse adaptation of Wace's Roman de Brut in a later, substantially abridged and reworked form whose diction is often modernized. The surviving manuscript is also severely damaged by the 1731 Cotton Library fire, so deliberate condensation, scribal revision, and later physical loss remain distinct explanatory layers.
